title: Chat Application
description: Design a real-time chat system supporting 1-on-1 and group messaging with message persistence, presence indicators, and media sharing.
requirements: Real-time 1-on-1 and group messaging (up to 1000 members)
Message persistence — users can load history when rejoining
Online/offline presence indicators with last seen timestamp
nonFunctionalReqs: < 200ms message delivery P99 for online users
Messages durable — never lost after server ack
Horizontal scalability — add WebSocket servers as needed

components.label: Client
components.javaNote: Mobile/web client maintains persistent WebSocket connection. Reconnects with exponential backoff on disconnect. Sends heartbeat every 30s. Falls back to SSE (Server-Sent Events) if WebSocket blocked.

components.label: WebSocket Server
components.javaNote: Spring WebSocket + STOMP. Each server holds thousands of concurrent connections in-memory. Stateful — connection is pinned to server. Load balancer uses IP hash or sticky sessions to route reconnects to same server.

components.label: Message Service
components.javaNote: Validates message, assigns monotonic message ID (Snowflake), persists to Cassandra, publishes MessageEvent to Redis Pub/Sub channel for the conversation. Returns ack to sender.

components.label:
Message DB
(Cassandra)
components.javaNote: Partition key: (conversation_id), clustering key: (message_id DESC). Wide-row model lets you fetch last N messages with single partition read. Tunable consistency: QUORUM for writes, ONE for reads (eventual). TTL = 1 year for message retention.

components.label: Presence Service
components.javaNote: Each WebSocket server publishes heartbeats to Redis SETEX 'presence:{userId}' 45s. Presence service subscribes to expiry events (notify-keyspace-events Ex) to detect going offline. Reads presence key to check online status.

components.label: Pub/Sub (Redis)
components.javaNote: Each conversation has a Redis channel: 'chat:{conversationId}'. WebSocket servers subscribe to channels for their connected users. On new message: publish to channel → all subscribed servers receive → push to connected users. Avoids direct server-to-server connections.

components.label: Media Storage (S3)
components.javaNote: Client uploads media directly to S3 via presigned URL (15 min expiry) bypassing chat servers. Message contains S3 object key. CDN (CloudFront) serves media with signed cookies for access control.

componentBreakdown.heading: WebSocket vs SSE vs Long Polling
componentBreakdown.detail: WebSocket is bidirectional, low overhead — ideal for chat. SSE is simpler (unidirectional, HTTP) — good for notifications. Long polling is highest latency and server load — avoid for chat.

componentBreakdown.heading: Message Ordering with Snowflake IDs
componentBreakdown.detail: Cassandra clustering column is message_id (Snowflake) in DESC order. Snowflake IDs are monotonically increasing so ORDER BY message_id gives chronological order. Fetch last 50 messages: WHERE conversation_id=? LIMIT 50.

componentBreakdown.heading: Fan-out via Redis Pub/Sub
componentBreakdown.detail: When Server A receives a message for a conversation, it publishes to Redis channel. Servers B and C subscribed to that channel deliver to their connected users. No direct server-to-server connections needed.
